g.data-narration: Resolution is a fundamental inference rule in logic that enables automated theorem proving and forms the basis of logic programming.
Resolution is an inference rule developed by Alan Robinson in 1965 that operates on clauses in conjunctive normal form to derive new conclusions.
g.text: A complete inference rule for propositional and first-order logic
• Operates on clauses in CNF (disjunction of literals)

g.data-narration: Resolution is crucial because it provides the theoretical foundation for Prolog, automated reasoning systems, and knowledge representation in AI.
g.text: • Powers expert systems and knowledge-based AI

g.data-narration: Before resolution, any formula must be converted to clause form through eight systematic steps including implication elimination and skolemization.
The resolution rule takes two clauses with complementary literals P and ¬P, and derives a new clause containing all other literals combined.
Unification finds substitutions that make two literals identical. The Most General Unifier (MGU) is the least restrictive binding that achieves this.
g.text: Finding substitutions to make literals identical:
If variable → apply occurs check, return substitution
Unify: P(x, f(y)) with P(g(a), f(b))

g.data-narration: In predicate logic, resolution combines unification with the resolution rule using the MGU to derive new clauses.
g.text: Using MGU θ to unify complementary literals L₁ and L₂:

g.data-narration: Resolution strategies control the search space: unit resolution uses single literals, set of support requires negated goal involvement, and linear resolution chains results.
Horn clauses have at most one positive literal and form the basis of logic programming, enabling efficient forward and backward chaining in Prolog.
g.text: Horn clause: at most ONE positive literal → basis of logic programming

g.data-narration: Refutation proves statements by contradiction: negate the goal, add to facts, and derive the empty clause to confirm the original statement.

g.text: Add all facts + negated goal as clauses

g.data-narration: In this example, we prove R from P→Q, Q→R, and P by converting to CNF, adding ¬R, and resolving to derive contradiction.
g.text: Example: Prove R from P→Q, Q→R, P

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: resolution is complete for first-order logic, empty clause means contradiction, and Horn clauses enable efficient logic programming in Prolog.

g.text: At most 1 positive literal → Prolog basis
Forgetting occurs check | Not negating goal | Skolemizing incorrectly
